content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance · · Administration · · Executive/Legal · · Enforcement · · Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5602                322-5660                322-5901              322-6441                    322-6444

February 7, 1983


Frederick K. Lowell
Pillsbury, Madison & Sutro
225 Bush Street
P.O. Box 7880
San Francisco, CA  94120

                                        Re: Our File No. A-83-012

Dear Mr. Lowell:

     In your letter of January 18, 1983, you asked a number of
questions concerning the scope of Government Code Section 84308*
("AB 1040") and the Commission's regulations interpreting that
section.  In particular, you asked about:

     (1)  The definition of "agent" in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18438.3(b);

     (2)  The inclusion of contributions to federal election
campaigns in the definition of contributions in 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18438.5(a);

     (3)  The provision for aggregation of contributions in
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.5(b);

     (4)  The applicability of Section 84308 to a situation in
which a partner of an individual, or a PAC of other members of a
trade association to which an individual belongs, make
contributions to members of a quasi-judicial board or commission
before which the individual has an application pending.

     Because you did not ask these questions on behalf of
specifically identified clients, and did not provide a

*All statutory references are to the Government Code unless
otherwise noted.

Frederick K. Lowell
February 7, 1983
Page 2


pecific factual context for the questions, this letter does not
constitute advice under Section 83114. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18329(b). However, I will try to answer your questions
in general terms in order to clarify the Commission's intent in
adopting its regulations, and to point out areas in which you
may wish to make requests for more specific advice under Section
83114 on behalf of your clients.

     1. Definition of Agent: You have asked whether an
architectural, engineering or law firm that provides "merely" provides
technical data which is included in an application is the
applicant's "agent" within the meaning of Section 84308 and
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.3(b). In general, unless such a
firm either appears before the board or commission in person, or
communicates directly with the members of the board or commis-
sion, we would not consider the firm to be an agent. However,
when a firm goes beyond "mere provision of technical data" --
for example, when an engineering firm prepares an application,
including written materials bearing the engineering firm's name
and advocating the applicant's position -- the firm might be
considered to be "directly communicating" with the board or
commission and thus to be an "agent" of the applicant. Clearly,
there will be many gradations of involvement by such firms in
applications to agencies covered by Section 84308. When you are
faced with a specific situation for a specific client, we will
be glad to provide you with more specific advice.

     2. Definition of Contributions: In your second question
you asked about 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.5(a), which
defines contributions for purposes of Section 84308. You
suggested that the inclusion of federal contributions in that
definition is preempted by 2 USC Section 453. In this respect I
suggest that you look at the Opinion of Counsel of the Federal
Elections Commission (OC 1975-14) concerning preemption of a
Massachusetts statute containing provisions similar to Section
84308. I have included a copy of the opinion for your con-
venience. The opinion concluded that a number of provisions
relating to contributions, including restrictions on the
solicitation and receipt of campaign contributions by appointed
state officials (Chapter 55, Mass. General Laws, Sections
11-17), were not preempted by 2 USC Section 453. In light of
this opinion it does not appear that there is any preemption
problem with the definition of contribution in 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18438.5(a); therefore there is no need to consider the
application of Article III, Section 3.5 of the California
Constitution to Section 84308 or to the Commission's regulation.

Frederick K. Lowell
February 7, 1983
Page 3


     You also asked whether the definition in 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18438.5(a) applies to both Section 84308(a) and Section
84308(b). 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.5(a) was intended to
provide a single definition of contribution for Section 84308,
and thus applies to Section 84308(a) as well as to Section
84308(b).

     3. Aggregation of Contributions: You raised two questions
regarding 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.5(b). First, you point
out that the statute refers to contributions from an applicant
or his or her agent, and question whether, in view of this
language, the statute permits any aggregation of contributions.
The word "or," while most often used as a disjunctive term, is
often used in statutes as a conjunctive term. See, e.g. De Sylva
v. Ballentine (1956) 76 S.Ct. 974, 976, 977, 351 US 570, 573,
575; 1002 Ed 1415. In fact, there are a number of other places
in the Political Reform Act where "or" is used in a conjunctive
sense. E.g. Section 84200(b) ("... in an odd-numbered year,
have made contributions or independent expenditures of five
hundred dollars ..."); Section 87103(a) ("Any business entity in
which the public official has a direct or indirect interest
worth more than one thousand dollars ...").

     The Commission has determined that the interpretation in
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.5(b) best carries out the
purposes of Section 84308. It would defeat the purposes of this
section if an applicant and his or her agents could cumulatively
contribute more than $250 to a board or commission member. The
Commission, of course, has the authority to construe the Act
liberally to accomplish its purposes. Section 81003.

     Your second question was whether the principle of
aggregation applies to both Section 84308(a) and Section
84308(b). Nothing in the regulation indicates that it should be
limited to Section 84308(a), and we see no policy reason for
such a narrow interpretation.

     4. Applicability to Collective Groups: Your last question
concerned the applicability of Section 84308 to collective
groups. As a general rule, the Commission has not interpreted
the conflict of interest provisions of the Act to require
disqualification under Section 87100 when a public official has
received income from a partner of an individual who will be
affected by a governmental decision, or from a PAC or other
members of a trade association of which such an individual is a
member. We anticipate taking the same general approach under
Section 84308 to questions of contributions made by partners of

Frederick K. Lowell
February 7, 1983
Page 4


an applicant, or by a PAC or other members of a trade asso-
ciation in which the applicant is a member.  However, I should
caution you that with respect to this question, as with your
question concerning agents, there may be a wide range of factual
situations, some of which could possibly trigger the provisions
of Section 84308.  Therefore, if you are unsure in a specific
case whether or not Section 84308 applies, I would suggest that
you ask for advice under Section 83114(b).

I hope this letter has been of assistance to you.  If you
have any further questions on Section 84308, please do not
hesitate to contct me.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Barbara K. Wilman
                                        Chief
                                        /Legal Division

BAM:plh
Attachments

LAW OFFICES OF

PILLSBURY, MADISON & SUTRO

225 BUSH STREET
POST OFFICE BOX 7880
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94120
TELEPHONE (415) 983-1000

LONDON
HÔTEL DE TALLEYRAND
LONDON SW1E 5 OU
TELEX 887658

CABLE ADDRESS "EVANS"
TELEX 34743
TELECOPIER (415) 398-2096

WRITER'S DIRECT DIAL NUMBER

(415) 983-1585

F P P C
JAN 19 12:40 P[illegible]

WASHINGTON, D.C.
1275 PENNSYLVANIA AVE., N.W.
WASHINGTON, D.C. 20036
TELEPHONE (202) 887-0300

LOS ANGELES
515 SOUTH FLOWER STREET
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90017
TELEPHONE (213) 629-2700

SAN JOSE
101 PARK CENTER PLAZA
SAN JOSE, CALIFORNIA 95113
TELEPHONE (408) 287-2533

January 18, 1983

Scope of A.B. 1040


Ms. Barbara A. Milman
Attorney at Law
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Barbara:

     Pursuant to Government Code section 83114(b), this letter constitutes a request for technical advice with respect to the scope of A.B. 1040 which added section 84308 to the Government Code effective January 1, 1983.

     I have the following questions:

     1. Definition of "Agent." Government Code section 84308(a) prohibits elected officers, alternates and candidates serving on quasi-judicial boards or commissions from accepting, soliciting or directing contributions of $250 or more from agents of applicants and agents of persons who actively support or oppose applications. Under regulations recently promulgated by the Commission, an "agent" is defined as only including a person who "represents" a principal in connection with the proceeding (2 Cal.Admin.Code, § 18438.3(b)). A question arises as to what "represents" means in this context. It seems clear that many applicants may use law, architectural, engineering or consulting firms in the course of applying for a permit before a quasi-judicial board or commission. Does the Commission take the position that the mere provision of technical data which is included in

an applicant's application is sufficient to render the
data provider an "agent" under the regulation or, does
a person only become an agent by appearing at a proceeding or engaging in direct communication (as defined in
the Act) with members of the board? The latter interpretation (i.e., requiring direct communication) is consistent
with the Commission's regulation defining "persons who
actively support or oppose" (2 Cal.Admin.Code, § 18438.4)
since such persons are only deemed to be those who communicate directly with members of the board or commission.

     2. Definition of "Contributions." Under section 18438.5(a) of the Commission's regulations, the term
"contributions" is defined as made for the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of the
voters in Federal as well as in state or local elections,
although presumably only for the purposes of Government
Code section 84308. The regulation by California of contributions to Federal office is preempted by the Federal
Election Campaign Act of 1971, as amended (2 U.S.C. § 431;
11 C.F.R. § 108.7). Assuming, however, that the Commission
is required by Article III, section 3.5 of the California
Constitution to ignore the fact of Federal preemption, is
it correct to infer from the language of the statute that
Federal contributions are only covered under section 84308(b)
and not under section 84308(a)?

     3. Aggregation of Contributions. Section
18438.5(b) of the Commission's regulations states that
in order to determine whether a contribution of $250 or
more has been made by a person or his or her agent, contributions made by that person within the preceding 12
months must be aggregated with those made by his or her
agent within the preceding 12 months or the period of the
agency relationship, whichever is shorter. This regulation appears to conflict with the language in section
84308(a) which prohibits a restricted person from accepting or soliciting a contribution of $250 or more from an
applicant or his or her agent. Similarly, section 84308(b)
requires disqualification of a member or board or commission if he or she has knowingly received a contribution of
$250 or more within the preceding 12 months from the applicant or his or her agent. I note further that it is not
clear whether the aggregation is meant only to apply for
the purposes of disqualification of a member under section
84308(b) or whether it is also meant to apply to the prohibition set forth in section 84308(a).

     4. Applicability to Collective Groups. An
additional question arises with respect to the applicability of this law to a collective group of persons. For
example, if a member of a trade association is an applicant for a permit and the trade association actively supports the application, does the limitation on contributions

2.

to the members of the board or commission apply to all of
the other members of the trade or membership association?
Does the limitation apply to the political action committee
of the trade or membership association? Similarly, in the
case of a partnership, if one partner is an applicant are
all the other partners subject to the limitation?

Please contact me if you have any questions con-
cerning this letter. Thank you for your attention on this
matter.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
Frederick K. Lowell

3.

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5602          322-5660          322-5901          322-6441          322-6444

February 8, 1983


Fred Lowell
Pillsbury, Madison & Sutro
225 Bush Street
San Francisco, CA    94120

Dear Fred:

     Thank you for your letter dated December 22, 1982, in which you asked for confirmation of my previous verbal advice regarding attempts to influence the Governor's appointments. Your specific question was whether such attempts constitute "legislative or administrative action" pursuant to the lobbying provisions of the Political Reform Act. Because you did not ask this question on behalf of a specifically identified client, this letter does not constitute advice under Section 83114 of the Government Code.

     The process of making appointments to state agencies is not defined as "legislative or administrative action" and, therefore, attempts to influence the Governor's decision on such appointments is not reportable by persons filing statements pursuant to Government Code Sections 86107 and 86108.

     You are, however, correct in your assumption that once a particular appointment has been made by the Governor, any attempts to influence the legislative approval process would constitute "legislative action" pursuant to Government Code Section 82037 which states in part that:

          "Legislative action" means the drafting, introduction, consideration, modification, enactment or defeat of any bill, resolution, amendment, report, nomination or other matter by the Legislature...

     If I can be of any further assistance, please feel free to contact me.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Lynn Montgomery
                                        Political Reform Consultant

LM/bo

LAW OFFICES OF

PILLSBURY, MADISON & SUTRO

LONDON                                                    225 BUSH STREET                                    WASHINGTON, D.C.
109 VICTORIA STREET                                                                                        1629 SEVENTEENTH STREET, N.W.
LONDON SWlE 6 QU                                    POST OFFICE BOX 7880                                  WASHINGTON, D.C. 20036
TELEPHONE (01) 930-8821                                                                                    TELEPHONE (202) 887-0300
                                                    SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94120
CABLE ADDRESS "EVANS"
TELEX 34743                                         TELEPHONE (415) 983-1000                             LOS ANGELES
TELECOPIER (415) 398-2096                                                                                 333 SOUTH HOPE STREET
                                                                                                           LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90017
WRITER'S DIRECT DIAL NUMBER                                                                               TELEPHONE (213) 629-9500

(415) 983-1585
                                                                                                           SAN JOSE
                                                                                                           55 ALMADEN BOULEVARD
                                                                                                           SAN JOSE, CALIFORNIA 95113
                                                                                                           TELEPHONE (408) 287-2333


                                                                        December 22, 1982


                                                    Political Reform Act of 1974 -
                                                    Lobbying Activities



Ms. Lynn Montgomery
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Lynn:

        Pursuant to our conversation yesterday, this
letter constitutes a request for technical advice with
respect to attempts to influence the Governor's appoint-
ments.

        I would appreciate it if you would confirm my
understanding that attempts to influence Governor-elect
Deukmejian's appointments to state agencies do not con-
stitute attempts to influence legislative or administra-
tive action within the scope of the lobbying disclosure
provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974. It is
my understanding that the action of the Governor in making
appointments to state offices does not constitute "legis-
lative action" because this is not a matter for considera-
tion by the legislature. Similarly, I understand that in-
fluencing the Governor's appointments does not constitute
influencing administrative action because the appointment
of an agency official does not constitute a quasi-legislative
or rate-making proceeding of a state agency.

        Notwithstanding the foregoing, I assume that when
a particular appointment is subject to legislative approval
and an attempt is made to influence the legislature, then

such activities would constitute influencing legislative
action under the Act.

I enjoyed talking with you yesterday in Sacramento
and I look forward to your response.

Very truly yours,

Frederick K. Lowell

2.

have made contributions or independent expenditures of five

which the public official has a direct or indirect interest

limi ted to Section 84308 (b), and we see no policy reason for

Applicability to Collective Groups: 
Your last question 
concerned the applicability of Section 84308 to collective 
groups. 
As a general rule, the Commission has not interpreted

an applicant, or by a PAC or other members of a trade asso-

sections.conclusion: General guidance provided on each topic: (1) A firm providing merely technical data would not be considered an agent unless it appears before the board/commission or communicates directly with members; (2) Federal contributions are not preempted under 2 USC Section 453 based on FEC Opinion OC 1975-14; the definition applies to both Section 84308(a) and (b); (3) Aggregation of contributions from an applicant and his/her agents is permitted and applies to both subsections; (4) As a general rule, contributions from partners or PAC members are not triggering disqualification, but specific factual situations may require individual advice.
sections.question_synthetic: The letter addresses four questions regarding the scope of Government Code Section 84308 (AB 1040): the definition of "agent" for purposes of contribution restrictions, whether federal election contributions are included in the definition of contributions, how contributions should be aggregated to determine if the $250 threshold is met, and whether the statute applies to contributions made by partners or members of trade associations of applicants.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC provided general guidance clarifying that: agents must directly communicate with the board/commission (not merely provide technical data); federal contributions are included and not preempted; contributions must be aggregated from both applicants and their agents to determine if the $250 threshold is crossed; and contributions from partners or trade association members generally do not trigger Section 84308 disqualification, though specific cases should seek individual advice.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request letter (from Pillsbury, Madison & Sutro dated January 18, 1983) and the FPPC response letter (from Chief Barbara A. Milman dated February 7, 1983). The FPPC response explicitly states it does not constitute formal advice under Section 83114 due to lack of specific client context, though general guidance is provided. OCR quality is good with minor typographical errors (e.g., 'contrioutions' instead of 'contributions').

h undr ed dollar s ... ") i Section 87103 (a) 
("any bus iness en ti ty in 
which the public official has a direct or indirect interest 
worth more than one thousand dollars .. :") . 
The Commission has determined that the interpretation in 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.5(b) best carries out the 
purposes of Section 84308. 
It would defeat the purposes of this 
section if an applicant and his or her agents could cumulatively 
contribute more than $250 to a board or commission member. 
The 
Commission, of course, has the authority to construe the Act 
liberally to accomplish its purposes. 
Section 81003. 
Your second question was whether the principle of 
aggregation applies to both Section 84308(a) and Section 
84308(b). 
Nothing in the regulation indicates that it should De 
limi ted to Section 84308 (b), and we see no policy reason for 
such a narrow interpretation. 
4. 
Applicability to Collective Groups: 
Your last question 
concerned the applicability of Section 84308 to collective 
groups. 
As a general rule, the Commission has not interpreted 
the conflict of interest provisions of the Act to require 
disqualification under Section 87100 when a public official has 
received income from a partner of an individual who will be 
affected by a governmental decision, or from a PAC or other 
m~mbers of a trade association of which such an individual is a 
m~mber. 
We anticipate taking the same general approach under 
Section 84308 to questions of contributions made 
partners of 


Frederick K. Lowell 
February 7, 1983  
an applicant, or by a PAC or other members of a trade asso-
ciation in which the applicant is a member. 
However, I should 
caution you that with respect to this question, as with your 
question concerning agents, there may be a wide range of factual 
situations, some of whicn could possibly trigger the provisions 
of Section 84308. 
Therefore, if you are unsure in a specific 
case wnether or not Section 84308 applies, I would suggest that 
you ask for advice under Section 83114(b).

CONCLUSION: General guidance provided on each topic: (1) A firm providing merely technical data would not be considered an agent unless it appears before the board/commission or communicates directly with members; (2) Federal contributions are not preempted under 2 USC Section 453 based on FEC Opinion OC 1975-14; the definition applies to both Section 84308(a) and (b); (3) Aggregation of contributions from an applicant and his/her agents is permitted and applies to both subsections; (4) As a general rule, contributions from partners or PAC members are not triggering disqualification, but specific factual situations may require individual advice.

BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance · · Administration · · Executive/Legal · · Enforcement · · Statements of Economic Interest (916) 322-5602 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444 February 7, 1983 Frederick K. Lowell Pillsbury, Madison & Sutro 225 Bush Street P.O. Box 7880 San Francisco, CA 94120 Re: Our File No. A-83-012 Dear Mr. Lowell: In your letter of January 18, 1983, you asked a number of questions concerning the scope of Government Code Section 84308* ("AB 1040") and the Commission's regulations interpreting that section. In particular, you asked about: (1) The definition of "agent" in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.3(b); (2) The inclusion of contributions to federal election campaigns in the definition of contributions in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.5(a); (3) The provision for aggregation of contributions in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.5(b); (4) The applicability of Section 84308 to a situation in which a partner of an individual, or a PAC of other members of a trade association to which an individual belongs, make contributions to members of a quasi-judicial board or commission before which the individual has an application pending. Because you did not ask these questions on behalf of specifically identified clients, and did not provide a *All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise noted. Frederick K. Lowell February 7, 1983 Page 2 pecific factual context for the questions, this letter does not constitute advice under Section 83114. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18329(b). However, I will try to answer your questions in general terms in order to clarify the Commission's intent in adopting its regulations, and to point out areas in which you may wish to make requests for more specific advice under Section 83114 on behalf of your clients. 1. Definition of Agent: You have asked whether an architectural, engineering or law firm that provides "merely" provides technical data which is included in an application is the applicant's "agent" within the meaning of Section 84308 and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.3(b). In general, unless such a firm either appears before the board or commission in person, or communicates directly with the members of the board or commis- sion, we would not consider the firm to be an agent. However, when a firm goes beyond "mere provision of technical data" -- for example, when an engineering firm prepares an application, including written materials bearing the engineering firm's name and advocating the applicant's position -- the firm might be considered to be "directly communicating" with the board or commission and thus to be an "agent" of the applicant. Clearly, there will be many gradations of involvement by such firms in applications to agencies covered by Section 84308. When you are faced with a specific situation for a specific client, we will be glad to provide you with more specific advice. 2. Definition of Contributions: In your second question you asked
embedding.summary: This is an FPPC advice letter responding to general questions about the scope and application of Government Code Section 84308 (AB 1040), which restricts contributions to members of quasi-judicial boards and commissions. The FPPC provided interpretive guidance on four key regulatory issues while noting that because the questions lacked specific client facts, the letter does not constitute formal advice under Section 83114.
